ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai stir-fried cabbage with roast pork. FOODON_00002809 (pork dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Thai stir-fried cabbage with roast pork. A0CKP (Pork, cooked) used as closest parent anchor for the dominant protein component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept identified for this specific Thai mixed dish.

main_ingredients: roast pork (mu daeng / char siu style)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of smoky stir-fried cabbage with roast pork is approximately 250 g, comprising a generous portion of wok-fried cabbage topped with sliced roast pork, as typically served in a Thai restaurant or hawker setting.

nutrient_source.title: Stir-fried cabbage with pork — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entries: Cabbage, raw (FDC ID 169975) and Pork, fresh, loin, whole, cooked, roasted (FDC ID 167903), combined in approximate 55:35 ratio with 10% oil/sauce allowance
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Thai stir-fried cabbage with roast pork exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: cabbage (raw, FDC 169975) at ~55% by weight, cooked roast pork loin (FDC 167903) at ~35% by weight, and cooking oil/sauce components at ~10%. Nutrient values scaled and blended proportionally. Energy cross-checked using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — stir-fried mixed vegetable and pork dish analogous estimate; cabbage GI reference from Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2021)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai stir-fried cabbage with roast pork. GI estimated at 32 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate contributors: cabbage (GI ~10, non-starchy vegetable, University of Sydney GI Database) and oyster/soy sauce (GI ~20–30 range). The pork component contributes negligible glycemic load. Blended dish GI estimated at 32 (low category). Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (32 × 5.8 g carbohydrate per 100g × 2.5) / 100 = 4.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sauce preparation, as a 250 g serving provides approximately 1200 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low net carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a low estimated GI of 32 and only 5.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g, this dish is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions, though the sodium content from sauces warrants attention in those with concurrent hypertension.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from roast pork
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~1200 mg per serving) and moderate saturated fat from roast pork (approximately 4.5 g per serving) are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose leaner pork cuts and request reduced-sauce preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Smoky Stir-Fried Cabbage with Roast Pork is a Thai-style wok dish featuring shredded or roughly chopped cabbage stir-fried over high heat with sliced roast pork (mu daeng), garlic, oyster sauce, fish sauce, soy sauce, and white pepper. The dish is characterised by the distinctive smoky flavour imparted by high-heat wok cooking (wok hei), producing lightly charred cabbage edges and tender-crisp texture. The roast pork contributes savoury, slightly sweet, and caramelised notes. It is commonly served as a side dish or main over steamed jasmine rice in Thai restaurants and hawker-style eateries across Southeast Asia, including Singapore. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate protein (~9.8 g per 100 g) from the pork component, low carbohydrate (~5.8 g per 100 g), and useful micronutrients including vitamin C from cabbage and B vitamins from pork. The primary clinical concern is sodium, which is elevated due to the combination of fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce used in seasoning. The glycemic index is estimated as low (~32), making it broadly suitable for glycaemic management. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with shellfish or fish allergies due to oyster sauce and fish sauce content.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Thai stir-fried cabbage with roast pork was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cabbage and cooked pork loin, blended proportionally. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database reference values for cabbage and sauce components. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central (FDC IDs 169975 and 167903) in approximate 55:35:10 ratio (cabbage:pork:oil/sauce). No direct approved-source entry exists for this specific dish. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entries used for this composite estimate. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central source data for the component ingredients. 4. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. Sodium is elevated (~480 mg per 100 g; ~1200 mg per 250 g serving) due to fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce seasoning typical of this dish style. 6. The food_name has been normalised from the promotional menu title 'Smoky Fire-Kissed Cabbage with Roast Pork' to the canonical culinary descriptor 'Smoky Stir-Fried Cabbage with Roast Pork'; the original menu name is preserved in aliases. 7. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA component data and are approximate. 8. Vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source entries used for this composite estimate.
